description:
You are assisting a monitoring-specialist who keeps two tiny health-check scripts in 
/home/user/monitoring/:

1. /home/user/monitoring/check_disk.sh  
   (prints a single line “DISK OK” to STDOUT)

2. /home/user/monitoring/check_memory.sh  
   (prints a single line “MEMORY OK” to STDOUT)

Right now the directory contains only those two scripts and nothing else.  
The specialist wants a Makefile that offers a very small automation layer over these
checks.  Your assignment is to author that Makefile and verify that it works.

Exact requirements for the Makefile (all file paths must be hard-coded, never relative):

A. Location  
   • The file must be created as  
     /home/user/monitoring/Makefile

B. Phony targets (exact, lowercase names)  
   1. all        – default, depends on “disk” and “memory” (so running “make” alone
                   executes both checks).  
   2. disk       – runs /home/user/monitoring/check_disk.sh and writes its output to  
                   /home/user/monitoring/logs/disk.log  
   3. memory     – runs /home/user/monitoring/check_memory.sh and writes its output to  
                   /home/user/monitoring/logs/memory.log  
   4. alert-log  – depends on both “disk” and “memory”. It must then create a new file
                   called  
                   /home/user/monitoring/logs/alert_TIMESTAMP.log  
                   where TIMESTAMP is in “YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS” format produced by the date(1)
                   command at execution time.  The file must contain exactly three lines:  
                     • line 1 – the same timestamp string used in the filename  
                     • line 2 – the single line contained in disk.log (i.e. “DISK OK”)  
                     • line 3 – the single line contained in memory.log (i.e. “MEMORY OK”)

C. Directory handling  
   • If /home/user/monitoring/logs does not exist, each rule that writes there must
     create it automatically.

D. Quietness  
   • The rules themselves may echo their own messages, but they must not rely on
     silent (-s) mode.  Human-readable output is fine.

E. Idempotency  
   • Successive invocations of “make alert-log” must always create a brand-new alert
     file with a fresh timestamp, without overwriting previous ones.

Validation instructions (what the automated test will do):

1. cd /home/user/monitoring  
2. make alert-log  
3. Locate the newest file whose name matches  
   alert_[0-9]\{8\}_[0-9]\{6\}\.log  
4. Confirm that its contents consist of exactly three newline-terminated lines that
   follow the format described in B-4.  
5. Confirm that disk.log contains “DISK OK\n”, memory.log contains “MEMORY OK\n”.

You do NOT need to modify the existing shell scripts.  
Do NOT hard-code any absolute timestamp; generate it at run time.

When finished, you may run the full build locally (optional) to prove that:
   make      → produces disk.log and memory.log  
   make alert-log → produces a properly formatted alert file

After implementing, you are done.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts:

Directory tree:
/home/user/monitoring/
    check_disk.sh
    check_memory.sh

File: /home/user/monitoring/check_disk.sh  
Permissions: 0755  
Contents (exact, including trailing newline):
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "DISK OK"

File: /home/user/monitoring/check_memory.sh  
Permissions: 0755  
Contents (exact, including trailing newline):
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "MEMORY OK"

Nothing else exists inside /home/user/monitoring/.

What the grader will verify after the agent’s actions:

1. /home/user/monitoring/Makefile exists with readable contents.
2. Running “make -C /home/user/monitoring disk”:
   • Exit status 0.  
   • File /home/user/monitoring/logs/disk.log exists and contains exactly
     DISK OK\n
3. Running “make -C /home/user/monitoring memory”:
   • Exit status 0.  
   • File /home/user/monitoring/logs/memory.log exists and contains exactly
     MEMORY OK\n
4. Running “make -C /home/user/monitoring alert-log”:
   • Exit status 0.  
   • A new file is created under /home/user/monitoring/logs whose name matches
     alert_[0-9]{8}_[0-9]{6}.log
   • That file contains three newline-terminated lines:
       a. The timestamp string (must match the timestamp embedded in the filename)
       b. DISK OK
       c. MEMORY OK
5. Re-running “make -C /home/user/monitoring alert-log” immediately afterwards
   produces a second alert_*.log file with a different timestamp, proving the
   recipe doesn’t overwrite the previous one.
